__fixture__.name: run_test_section_slot
__fixture__.scenario: Run/Test owns executable target browsing in the sidebar while terminal output, debug console, test output, and execution evidence use the bottom panel or main workspace. Start/stop/attach actions are command-backed and cannot be rail-only or private-sidebar-only.

owner_summary: Run/Test owns executable target browsing: run configurations, test tree, debug targets, task summaries, and execution setup.

forbidden_uses: The Run/Test rail entry cannot directly start, stop, rerun, attach, debug, or mutate execution context.
The Run/Test rail entry cannot show test result rows, debug controls, or terminal output.

forbidden_private_actions: Start, stop, rerun, attach, debug, and remote execution commands require command descriptors with execution-context truth.
Execution output and live consoles do not render as a private nested sidebar.
sidebar_contract.count_policy: Run/Test section counts distinguish configured targets, discovered tests, running sessions, failed sessions, blocked sessions, and stale discovery using collection and execution truth.

other_lane_boundaries.boundary_summary: Terminal output, test output, logs, debug console, and longitudinal task state live in bottom-panel tabs.
Debug views, coverage dashboards, failure review, and execution evidence that need sustained space open in the main workspace.
Execution-context metadata, provider state, and run configuration explanations may open in the inspector.
Attach, remote execution, and trust-sensitive target changes use sheets or review surfaces when preview or approval is required.

required_command_bindings.notes: Run/Test jump opens or restores the executable-target sidebar.
Starting a selected target is command-backed and must carry execution-context enablement, preview, and disabled-reason truth.

duplicate_surface_guardrails: A test-results tree does not become a second primary rail section; it is a Run/Test subsection or bottom-panel/main-workspace surface depending on task depth.
A debugger extension contributes targets or panes through Run/Test, bottom panel, inspector, or main workspace rather than a new global sidebar.
Output-over-time surfaces never replace the sidebar's structural target browser.
